title: Escalation Path Audit
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident:audit" field_name="assignment_group"
| eval from_group=old_value, to_group=new_value
| eval step=from_group."->".to_group
| stats values(step) as escalation_path, count as hops by number
| join max=1 number [ search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" priority IN (1,2) earliest=-90d | fields number, priority ]
| where hops>4
| table number, priority, escalation_path, hops
description: Audits whether incidents followed the documented escalation chain (L1→L2→vendor) for compliance and training.
value: Audits whether incidents followed the documented escalation chain (L1→L2→vendor) for compliance and training.
implementation: Build assignment hop strings from audit `old_value`/`new_value`. Flag incidents with excessive hops for P1/P2 (policy threshold). Optionally `lookup escalation_policy.csv` with first/last hop pairs for stricter audits. Adjust field names to match `sys_audit` extractions.
visualization: Table (unexpected paths), Sankey (escalation flow), Single value (audit exceptions).
knownFalsePositives: Reassignments from severity changes, support model cutovers, or a very large event touching many groups.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_snow`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Incident history / audit (`sourcetype=snow:incident:audit`).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Build assignment hop strings from audit `old_value`/`new_value`. Flag incidents with excessive hops for P1/P2 (policy threshold). Optionally `lookup escalation_policy.csv` with first/last hop pairs for stricter audits. Adjust field names to match `sys_audit` extractions.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident:audit" field_name="assignment_group"
| eval from_group=old_value, to_group=new_value
| eval step=from_group."->".to_group
| stats values(step) as escalation_path, count as hops by number
| join max=1 number [ search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" priority IN (1,2) earliest=-90d | fields number, priority ]
| where hops>4
| table number, priority, escalation_path, hops
```

Understanding this SPL

**Escalation Path Audit** — Audits whether incidents followed the documented escalation chain (L1→L2→vendor) for compliance and training.

Documented **Data sources**: Incident history / audit (`sourcetype=snow:incident:audit`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_snow`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: snow:incident:audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype="snow:incident:audit". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **from_group** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **step** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by number** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where hops>4` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Escalation Path Audit**): table number, priority, escalation_path, hops


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ServiceNow list or form that matches this data (for example the incident, request, or problem view). Reconcile a few record numbers, states, and times. Confirm the add-on poll or export time lines up with what you see in the browser.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unexpected paths), Sankey (escalation flow), Single value (audit exceptions).
grandmaExplanation: We review whether escalations follow the path we agreed, so the right people get pulled in and nobody is skipped by accident.
